children.name: Sarothrura
children.description: Small, secretive rails with a distinctive fluffy tail that is often cocked upright. Males and females often show striking sexual dimorphism, with males more colourful and females more cryptic. They are found across sub-Saharan Africa and Madagascar.

children.name: Sarothrura pulchra
children.description: A striking flufftail with a bright chestnut head and breast, black upperparts heavily spotted with white, and a short, cocked tail. It inhabits dense undergrowth along forest streams and swamps from Senegal to Uganda and south to Angola. Its call is a low, resonant hoot repeated at intervals.

children.name: Sarothrura elegans
children.description: The most widespread flufftail, found from Ethiopia to South Africa in forested and well-wooded habitats. The male has a rich chestnut head and breast with black upperparts finely spotted with buff. Its distinctive call is a soft, repeated 'hoot-hoot-hoot' accelerating into a trill.

children.name: Sarothrura rufa
children.description: A small flufftail with a bright rufous-chestnut head, neck and breast, a black-and-white spotted back and a short black tail. It favours dense marshes and wetlands with tall sedges and grasses across eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Sarothrura lugens
children.description: A flufftail with a chestnut head and breast, dark upperparts spotted with white, and a short black tail. Found in marshes and wet grasslands of Central and East Africa from Nigeria to Uganda and south to Angola. It is secretive and more often heard than seen, with a low-pitched hooting call.

children.name: Sarothrura affinis
children.description: An elusive flufftail with a bright chestnut head and breast and a boldly black-and-white striped back, found in the highland grasslands and moorlands of eastern and southern Africa. It occurs at elevations between 1,500 and 3,000 metres in damp, tussocky habitats.

children.name: Sarothrura ayresi
children.description: A small, threatened flufftail of high-altitude wetlands in Ethiopia, Zimbabwe, and South Africa. The male has a chestnut head and breast with a black-and-white barred back. It inhabits tussocky marshes and wet grasslands above 2,000 metres. It is classified as Endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Sarothrura watersi
children.description: A secretive flufftail endemic to the marshes of eastern Madagascar, with a chestnut head and breast, black upperparts finely spotted with white, and notably long toes adapted for walking on floating vegetation. It is classified as Endangered due to wetland destruction.

children.name: Sarothrura novae
children.description: This newly discovered species is a small, plump bird with soft downy feathers. It feeds on insects and fruits in dense forest understory.

children.name: Mentocrex hirsuteus
children.description: This species has a distinctive downy appearance, with feathers often tipped in long, curly hairs. It feeds on nectar and small insects.

children.name: Sarothrura pallidula
children.description: The Pale Flufftail has a striking pale plumage, almost white, with subtle barring on the underparts. It feeds primarily on aquatic insects and small crustaceans.

children.name: Mentocrex leucophrys
children.description: Distinctively marked with white plumage and contrasting dark streaks, this species forages in the canopy of tall trees. It feeds on fruit and nectar.

children.name: Sarothrura nocturna
children.description: This species is active at night, with cryptic plumage that blends perfectly into the dark forest floor. It feeds primarily on insects it detects using its acute sense of hearing.

children.name: Mentocrex rufusfasciatus
children.description: This species is named for the striking dark stripes on its white underparts. It feeds on small amphibians and aquatic invertebrates.

children.name: Mentocrex
children.description: A small genus of forest-dwelling rails endemic to Madagascar, placed in the flufftail family based on genetic evidence. Unlike most flufftails, they lack the distinctive fluffy tail and are more terrestrial in habit.

children.name: Mentocrex kioloides
children.description: A medium-sized rail with olive-brown upperparts, a grey breast and head, and a distinctive white throat patch. It is endemic to the humid forests of eastern Madagascar, where it skulks on the forest floor in dense undergrowth. It feeds on insects, seeds, and small invertebrates, and its call is a series of sharp, whistled notes.

children.name: Mentocrex beankaensis
children.description: A recently described rail endemic to the dry tsingy limestone forests of western Madagascar. It has olive-brown upperparts, a grey breast, and a pale throat. It is distinguished from the Madagascar Wood Rail by genetic differences and subtle plumage variations. It inhabits the unique karst limestone forest formations of the Bemaraha region.
